text: Welcome to This is My Architecture.
I'm Morit and with me Liron from Deep Instinct.
Liron, tell us about Deep Instinct.
Well, Deep Instinct applies deep learning to cybersecurity.
We use it for real-time detection and prevention of zero-day attacks.
Cool. What are we going to talk about today?
So, providing the zero-day attack prevention,
we will need to use agent on clients in order to provide that.
We use to create the environment in order to provide millions of agents
that will want to access the web and get information from the web.
Now, we use CloudFront in order to provide all this information.
Now, the agent will get a request directly from CloudFront
and then CloudFront will use S3 in order to provide the information back.
Now, what we found that we wanted to create a real-time statistics solution.
The problem is normally that CloudFront will provide logs, which logs are amazing.
They are good, but they are not good for real-time statistics.
In order to provide real-time statistics, we had to use Lambda at Edge,
which can provide us a real-time solution for the request from CloudFront.
The idea was like that.
Every agent will ask hundreds of millions of times a day information from the CloudFront.
All the agents together will request everything and we can tap it in the middle of the request,
use Lambda at Edge at the viewer request situation,
take all this information and inject it directly into DynamoDB in order to process it in real-time.
From all the purpose-built databases out there today, why did you choose DynamoDB?
It's easy, simple, we can change it on the fly and change anything we want for the statistics without any hindrance.
Sounds good. Are there any other use cases you use Lambda at Edge for?
Okay, so after we did this, we understood the major implementation and changes that we can do onto our system.
What we try to do is create a rolling upgrade to the agent.
Now, an agent will request an upgrade and normally it will go to S3 to get the upgrade.
But if we don't want millions of agents to get the update at the same time,
we want to gradually distribute it around the world, we can tackle the request at the viewer request,
log anything to another DynamoDB, and then see if the request became, for example, 10,000, 100,000,
and then stop it. Use a viewer request to respond to the agent. There is no update right now.
How do you uniquely identify your agent?
Okay, so in order to do that, we actually used something called signed request to the CloudFront.
Normal signed request is used for security measures, so other one than the agent will not be able to go to the CloudFront.
What we did, we used to inject a unique identifier inside the request in order to read it.
Thus, the viewer request at the Lambda at Edge will get the information of which agent directly.
This unique information will provide us even rate limit monitor in order to provide,
if we see the same request with the same identifier go from different IP addresses,
then we can know this is a problem.
Also, this will help us get all the statistics go to the DynamoDB,
because if customer is under not, we cannot see all the agents.
With this unique identifier, we can recognize any agent by itself and provide any solution for it.
Cool. It looks like you leverage Lambda at Edge to collect real-time statistics in an efficient way,
and also to intervene in the middle of the request before it reaches the origin,
and keep the details about the specific unique agent.
Not only that, what we found out, that we can provide a real-time decision in the Lambda at Edge, the viewer request.
What we found out, that if we want to do any change to the information that gets back into the agent,
we can intervene in something called Lambda at Edge original response.
Once the request comes to the viewer request, it will then go and ask S3 for the information.
But the original response does not know that the viewer request did this information.
To do that, we logged all the information we wanted into DynamoDB,
and then the original response will get this information back.
What we can do here is we can identify the agent because of the unique identifier.
We use it to save the state and know this agent requested a specific file and change it after we got it from the bucket.
So, gradual roll-outs of Deep Instinct goes through Lambda at Edge as well.
Yes, we can manipulate any updates, different updates for different customers, and everything according to that.
Amazing. Thanks Liron for sharing that with us, and thank you for watching This is My Architecture.
Thank you for watching.
